dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a possible large laceration with heavy bleeding near 12th and Broad.
Hi sir, this is Drew. I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What caused you to call 911 today?
I accidentally cut my hand real bad with a miter saw about 25 minutes ago.
He's been bleeding for a while. The tourniquet we made out of wire helped, but the blood keeps coming.
What's your name, and where are we at right now?
My name's Rick. I think we're in front of my apartment on 12th street.
Excellent, and what day today is it?
Monday, May 6th, I think?
And what happened that brought us here?
Yeah, I was cutting some wood with a miter saw and it slipped and hit my hand.
Hello Rick. We're going to make a few quick measurements now to check your condition.
Blood pressure is 89 over 50. Let's take some more vital signs as we proceed.
Do you have any trouble breathing or choking right now?
No, I'm okay to breathe, just hurts really bad in the hand.
Can you lift your head and look at me for a moment, please?
Yeah.
Your airway is clear and open. That's great.
Can you take a full breath in and out for me?
Uh huh.
Breathing is at 20 a minute.
Chest rise and fall look equal and even.
SpO₂ is 96%.
Is this affected hand the left or right one?
Left.
Got a regular but slow radial pulse of 51 in the right arm.
His skin is pale, cool, and clammy. We'll continue to monitor.
Let me double-check your awareness. What is your name again?
Rick.
And the day of the week?
Monday, May 6th.
You seem alert and oriented. What is the month and year?
May of 2019.
That's excellent. He's A&O x4.
Rick, can you tell me about any medical conditions you have?
I have bipolar disorder. That's it.
That's good to know. Any medications you're currently taking for that?
Yeah, Xanax and Prozac.
Any allergies at all, medications or otherwise?
No, I've never had any major reactions.
Apart from the hand, how do you feel elsewhere?
I feel bottomed out, but the pain is only in my hand.
That's fine. We'll keep looking for anything else. Your skin seems pale and cool.
Rick, your pupils are equal and reactive to light. That's a good sign.
No jugular venous distension noted.
Trachea is midline with no signs of deviation or trauma to the neck.
Since we're still on the scene, we just rechecked your breathing. This time it was 14 per minute.
Oxygen saturation remains stable at 97%.
Also, note that your respiratory rate earlier was 20, and now it is at 14.
Blood pressure is now back to 108 over 66. So we are seeing some signs of improvement.
The pulse has gone down to 70. That's better than the 51 it was at first.
Rick, let's check what we can about your breathing again.
Slight retractions but otherwise no signs of airway obstruction.
It seems like your breathing is still unlabored and without distress.
Clear lung sounds bilaterally with no wheezing or crackles.
We plan to apply direct pressure on the wound now to further assess the bleeding control.
Your initial tourniquet was helpful. We'll dress the wound with a pressure dressing.
There's a large wound on the back side of your left hand. We don't know how deep it is due to ongoing bleeding.
We've started an IV in one of your veins to ensure fluids can be delivered quickly if needed.
Based on your faintness and skin appearance, we're going to give you some normal saline.
You'll get around 125 milliliters of normal saline in a few minutes. That should help raise your blood pressure gradually.
Rick, I'm going to place leads on your chest for monitoring. This is a standard part of care.
The initial read from the machine shows a sinus bradycardia.
It looks like the first 4-lead ECG monitoring was unsuccessful, but it worked later.
We'll do a quick check for blood sugar even though it came back at zero.
Your EKG remains showing sinus bradycardia at the moment.
Rick, we also did a general skin exam and found no bruising, rashes, or other skin issues besides the hand.
Pupils were equal and reactive again in the last assessment. No signs of trauma or bleeding in the head or neck.
Your neck was checked for stiffness, but no signs of nuchal rigidity were found.
Your mouth, ears, nose, and throat seem unremarkable. No signs of orally draining from head injuries.
We listened once more for heart and breath sounds. All normal despite sinus bradycardia on EKG.
Abdomen is soft and non-tender. No signs of distension or rigidity.
No masses or any signs of internal abdominal injuries.
We checked your left hand again-no fractures observed, but you can't move the second and third fingers.
We did not assess the lower extremities in detail, given the situation and resources.
No signs of genitourinary bleeding, and your urination history is normal up to the event.
We've secured your hand and applied another pressure dressing to help stop the bleeding.
You'll be loaded into the ambulance shortly and moved safely to the ER.
You will be transported to MCV Medical Center, specifically the ER, for further treatment.
We'll confirm with the ER staff when we arrive so they are ready to receive you.
Let's run through the vital signs again before heading out.
Blood pressure is 102 over 63. Your pulse is a stable 69 now.
Your breathing is still within a good range-16 breaths per minute.
You're still at 94% oxygen saturation, which is acceptable.
We'll check again after a few minutes en route and adjust as needed.
Rick, how would you rate the pain in your hand now, on a scale from 0 to 10?
It's at zero right now, since the bleeding is better and I can't really focus on it.
Nice work with the tourniquet and initial care. Let's keep you comfortable during transport.
Your gauze is soaked from earlier, but the new dressing has held steady.
You were soaking wet earlier, but now you're dry and your color has improved.
You were feeling very low before, but now you say you feel better since the fluids started to help you up.
The ECG shows stable sinus bradycardia, and your body signs continue to improve as we head in.
Your IV remains secure and fluids are going in as planned.
Once we arrive, we'll assist you off of the stretcher again and place you in a hospital bed.
Your current location was at the side of the street, and now you'll be arriving at MCV ER.
You've done well so far, and the ER is ready to see you right away once we arrive.
GCS is 15, with eye = 4, verbal = 5, and motor = 6. Your GCS is stable.
Respiratory rate is now 16 per minute. That's a normal and steady rate.
Trachea remains midline. No structural shift in your airway.
Your pupils are still PEARL-equal, round, and reactive to light.
No signs of jugular venous distension. Neck veins appear unremarkable.
We'll keep monitoring your vitals every 5 minutes during transport.
I've also set the monitor to alert and zones to check for any further heart issues or instability.
Our trip should be brief and uneventful. But we'll still stay on close watch in case of any dynamic changes.
We're ready to roll once emergency permitting and after receiving a full report from the crew.
The patient is set for transport, and we're being clear with the hospital about current status and history.
Since we're checking again, how do you feel in terms of overall pain now?
Possibly still on the low end, but I think it's the same as before-no additional pain right now.
Great. You remain at a zero, so no need for pain medication unless it gets worse.
On site analysis supports transport to the ER listed.
History and symptoms were consistent with a significant laceration.
In addition, the patient showed signs of hypotension initially, now trending up post-NS bolus.
The patient's wounds and general appearance justify ER evaluation.
IV was started in an extremity vein-arm or leg. Glucose checked as per protocol.
The protocol used was "injury - general trauma management (protocol 4 -1)".
